exemplar.title: What effect will a six-week plyometrics training programme have on the performance of recreational athletes (aged 15-18) middle-distance running time in 800m?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title: What effect will a six-week plyometrics training programme have on the performance of recreational athletes (aged 15-18) middle-distance running time in 800m?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: Topic and research question are communicated clearly and consistently throughout the report.
Purpose, population and performance variables are unambiguously framed, maintaining strong focus.
Hypotheses are stated using proper statistical notation and control variables are comprehensively listed.
weaknesses: Minor linguistic inaccuracies (e.g. âbiassedâ, âstraight trainingâ) occasionally impede the flow.
Sample size is small (n=15) and methodological limitations (no blinding, single post-measure) weaken completeness.
Independent variable description remains somewhat vague and could be specified more precisely.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work clearly fits the 5â6 band: the topic and research question are accurately and effectively communicated, and the methodology is generally appropriate. However, small lapsesâoccasional language errors, very limited sample, and incomplete methodological justificationâprevent a flawless performance. A score of 5 out of 6 best reflects these strengths balanced with the noted weaknesses.

strengths: Consistent and accurate use of specialist terminology (e.g. plyometrics, anaerobic glycolysis, RCT).
Extensive and current literature engagement with mostly relevant application of source material.
Detailed example calculations enhance transparency of analysis.
weaknesses: Occasional conceptual simplifications and inaccuracies (e.g. confusing speed units, sign convention issues).
Minor spelling errors do not alter meaning but slightly detract from precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Subject knowledge is good, with mostly relevant and current applications of SEHS concepts, placing it solidly in the 3â4 band but with consistent, accurate use of terminology justifying an upward placement. Overall, the balance of strong concept application and minor inaccuracies merits 5 out of 6.

strengths: Thorough data analysis using appropriate statistical tests, linking results back to the hypothesis and literature.
Well-structured argument in IMRAD format, comparing plyometric training with endurance training.
Evaluation section identifies key limitations (sample size, timing accuracy) and proposes reasonable improvements.
weaknesses: Some conclusions are overstated (claiming equal effectiveness despite non-significant differences).
Critical evaluation remains partly descriptive; deeper interrogation of blinding and measurement consistency is needed.
Misinterpretations in statistical discussion (e.g. p-value logic) indicate areas for refinement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate demonstrates strong research analysis (7/9 band) and coherent argument development (6/6 band), with a partially critical evaluation (4/6 band). While not consistently at the highest level of critical sophistication, the work surpasses the middle band. A holistic score of 9 out of 12 appropriately reflects the excellent analysis and argumentation tempered by evaluation shortcomings.

strengths: Clearly appropriate overall structure, following EE conventions (title page, sections, tables, figures, appendices).
Logical flow and comprehensive table of contents enhance navigation and coherence.
Consistent formatting of headings, captions, and references for the most part.
weaknesses: Minor layout issues (inconsistent spacing, some blurred images) detract slightly from professionalism.
Typographical and formatting errors (inconsistent unit formatting, occasional missing legends) interrupt reader accessibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Presentation criterion meets the 3â4 band: structure is exemplary and conventions are fully observed, but minor technical and formatting errors prevent perfection. Awarding the maximum 4 marks reflects the very high quality of presentation with only small areas for improvement.

strandSelections.reasoning: The evaluation section identifies sample size, timing accuracy, lack of error bars and duration as limitations and proposes reasonable improvements.  However, critique is largely descriptive and occasionally superficial; methodological weaknesses (e.g., absence of true blinding) are not deeply interrogated.  This fits âpartially critical evaluationâ, situated mid-band (score 4) within 4-6.

The research question is explicitly stated (âWhat effect will a six-week plyometrics training programme have â¦ on 800 m time?â).  It is sufficiently narrow (single intervention, age range, distance, duration) and is used to structure background, hypothesis, variables and statistical tests, demonstrating effective connection to the discussion.  Because the wording is precise and the rest of the report repeatedly links back to the question, the descriptor for 5-6 is met; a score of 5 recognises very good, though not perfect, focus.

Pagination, headings and table formatting are present, and most visuals are captioned.  Minor technical issues exist (some images slightly blurred, inconsistent unit formatting, negative signs for improvements).  Citation style is mostly consistent but not flawlessly applied.  These minor shortcomings drop the work just below perfect, earning 3 in the 3-4 band.

The essay contains a title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, labelled sections, figures, tables, references and appendices, following EE conventions.  Logical flow of introduction-methods-results-discussion is maintained without missing elements, meeting the âclearly appropriate structureâ descriptor; maximum 4 marks are justified.

The topic (plyometric training effects on 800 m performance in recreational 15-18-year-olds) is announced in the title page, reiterated in the introduction and consistently referenced through subsections, tables and graphs.  Purpose, population and performance variable are all unambiguously framed, allowing the reader to follow the study without confusion.  Minor linguistic inaccuracies (âbiassedâ, âstraight trainingâ) occasionally impede flow, preventing a flawless presentation, but overall communication remains clear and focused, justifying placement in the 5-6 band; a mid-band score of 5 best reflects these small lapses.

The essay integrates SEHS concepts such as stretch-shortening cycle, neuromuscular efficiency, VOâ max and energy system contributions, and applies them to justify the intervention and interpret results.  Citations are current and mostly accurate.  Some conceptual inaccuracies (confusing speed units, negative sign for speed gain, minor physiological simplifications) prevent âexcellentâ consistency, so the work aligns with âgood knowledge with mostly relevant applicationâ, warranting 4 marks in the 3-4 band.

Specialist terminology (plyometrics, anaerobic glycolysis, paired/unpaired t-test, stretch-shortening cycle, randomised control trial) is used accurately and consistently across sections, figures and appendices.  Occasional spelling errors do not alter meaning.  Hence the descriptor for accurate and consistent use is satisfied; a mid-band score of 5 reflects the solid but not exemplary precision.

The student processes raw and mean data, employs appropriate statistical tests, and links findings to the hypothesis and literature.  Analysis is clearly relevant and generally logical, though depth is limited by small sample and one performance metric.  Occasional misinterpretations (reporting negative speed changes, confusion over p-value wording) lower sophistication.  Overall quality aligns with âgood research analysis clearly relevantâ, edging into the lower end of the 7-9 band; 7 is awarded.

The student outlines an RCT, details apparatus, variables, ethical steps and statistical approach, and cites extensive literature.  Nonetheless, methodological completeness is weakened by a very small sample (n = 15), single post-measure, no blinding, and reliance on manual timing.  Source selection is relevant but not always critically justified.  These limitations fit the âmostly complete with generally relevant sources and some evidence of informed selectionâ description, placing the work at the top of the 3-4 band (score 4), rather than the fully âappropriate range and clear evidenceâ demanded for 5-6.

The report follows a coherent IMRAD structure, develops arguments that compare PT with endurance training and references supporting studies.  Reasoning is generally sound, yet some conclusions (e.g., claiming equal effectiveness despite non-significant difference) are not fully justified, and counter-arguments are lightly explored.  This indicates a well-developed but not âexcellentâ argument, placing the work at the top of the 4-6 band (score 6).

annotations.comment: References are extensive and current, demonstrating thorough literature engagement.
Citation of Stellingwerff et al. (2019) is appropriate, demonstrating good use of recent literature to justify training specificity.
The summary of Blagrove et al. (2018) lacks critique of variable control. Discuss how their methodological limitations inform your design.
The introduction to background uses âbiassedââuse standard spelling (âbiasedâ) to maintain professionalism.
The title specifies muscle strength but the report focuses on 800 m performance, creating a mismatch in topic framing. Ensure title and research focus align exactly to maintain clarity and coherence.
Word count is provided and falls within the 4,000-word limit, satisfying formatting requirements.
The introductory sentence is grammatically unclear (âneeds to approach specific. complex trainingâ), which disrupts flow. Rephrase for clarity.
An inline math fragment (\overline{2}) appears in isolation, suggesting a formatting or OCR error. Verify and correct math displays.
The word âarquesâ is a typo for âarguesâ, which undermines academic tone. Proofread for similar errors.
Research question is clearly restated at the end of the review, reinforcing focus.
The student effectively states the research question and connects it to the introduction, framing the investigation focus clearly.
The table of contents is thorough and follows conventions, aiding navigation and reflecting a clear structure.
Consider adding dot leaders or consistent spacing in the TOC entries to improve presentation and reader accessibility.
The personal interest section shows genuine engagement but would benefit from linking reflections to planning decisions.
The independent variable description is vague (âtype of trainingâ). Specify differences more precisely to strengthen methodology.
Definition of middle-distance running and energy contributions shows sound subject knowledge with relevant citation (Duffield et al., 2005).
Figure lacks a clear caption or legend explaining axes; add descriptive labels to improve reader interpretation.
Terminology like âanaerobic glycolysisâ and âstretch-shortening cycleâ is consistently accurate, demonstrating strong use of concepts.
Sample size (n=15) is small; discuss implications on statistical power here rather than only in the evaluation.
Raw and processed data tables are detailed but could be condensed by focusing on key participants to improve readability.
Hypotheses are stated using proper statistical notation (H_0, H_1), reflecting methodological rigor.
Control variables list is comprehensive, reflecting awareness of factors influencing results and enhancing reliability.
The PAR-Q appendix is detailed and ethical steps are clear, but could summarize key points in the main text for conciseness.
Description of RCT design demonstrates good methodological understanding and reduction of biases.
Example calculation of speed difference shows correct application of equations, enhancing transparency of analysis.
Negative sign used for speed improvement is confusing; consider clarifying sign convention (e.g., absolute gain) to avoid misinterpretation.
Graph axes should include error bars or confidence intervals to convey variability, strengthening the analysis.
Explanation of unpaired t-test rationale is instructive but misstates p-value threshold logic (âp-value 0.238 lower than 0.05â). Clarify interpretation.
Evaluation identifies key limitations and suggests reasonable improvements, but could delve deeper into blinding and measurement consistency.
The conclusion reflects results accurately, discussing both statistical findings and practical implications.
